context.description: Reflective prose argues that the self is shaped by the body as a whole, not mind alone, and questions the degree of control we have over ourselves. It frames human helplessness against intertwined bodily systems while still claiming that more capacity may be hidden in us if the body is properly nourished. The section heading asks where to locate leverage within these interdependent systems.
normalization_notes: Chapter or work title not visible on this page.

rows.paragraph_text: If we cannot fundamentally change this balance, how can I assume I have any kind of control over myself? Again I must ask who is really in charge here.
The three systems discussed so far are so intertwined and influence each other on so many levels that where could one even begin to intervene?

If we are truly honest with ourselves and ask, in the face of these three giants, who we really are, then we must admit that what we feel as our self is the sum total of all the activities of what we call our body, not only the brain but everything from the little toe to the hair roots.
We must also admit that it is not we who decide who we are and then the body follows this diktat, but rather the body determines the form of the self through the way it functions.
I am by no means an advocate of the idea that there is only matter and nothing else, but I also want to make clear that matter can do far more than we may suspect.

What has become clear to me over the years, and what I want to express in this book, is how much remains hidden in us and what our mind is actually capable of when the physical body it is linked to is properly nourished.
We may be pitifully helpless in our present state, but that does not mean it must remain that way.
It also does not help us to deny our helplessness just because we dislike it.
I have come to the insight that the mind cannot triumph over the body, at least not to the extent of gaining rule over the flesh at its roots.
The idea that the little fellow on the shoulder of this giant could force it to do anything would be far too presumptuous.
How could it? This giant offers us so many possibilities, of which we have so far made only sparse use, why not simply accept its offer and begin using its resources.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy philosophy_of_consciousness Book page from a German nonfiction philosophy work on consciousness and selfhood. Section heading: Where Do We Stand? Arguments focus on the body as the basis of the self, limited mind-body control, hidden human capacities, and the need to use bodily resources more fully.
